content:
@John Onstead wrote:

So I have these starter questions: first, what is the relationship between a chain complex and a simplicial object in an additive category (an Ab-enriched category with finite coproducts)?

Welcome to the Dold-Kan correspondence. This is worth getting to know well, especially since it holds a subtlety: the equivalence between chain complexes in AbGp\mathsf{AbGp}AbGp and simplicial abelian groups is both lax monoidal and oplax monoidal in both directions, but not strong monoidal.
I think the Dold-Kan correspondence, if anyone wants to talk about it, deserves its own thread. So here's that thread!

The forgetful functor AbGp→Set\mathsf{AbGp}\to\mathsf{Set}AbGp→Set induces a commutative monad on Set\mathsf{Set}Set whose algebras are the abelian groups. This automatically gives us the tensor product of algebras over a commutative monad and internal hom of algebras over a commutative monad, which are the usual tensor product and hom of abelian groups.

But I know that the internal hom of two bounded chain complexes can be unbounded. So what goes wrong when we try to repeat this argument with AbGpΔop→SetΔop\mathsf{AbGp}^{\Delta^\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}^{\Delta^\mathrm{op}}AbGpΔop→SetΔop?

Well, (1), as John already signaled us to be ready for, the equivalence of simplicial abelian groups with chain complexes bounded below is not strong monoidal! So the categories are the same but the monoidal categories aren’t and we shouldn’t conflate them. And, (2), I don’t think that’s true with the definition of tensor product of complexes I know, where the nnn th term of the tensor is the sum of Ai⊗BjA_i\otimes B_jAi​⊗Bj​ for i+j=n.i+j=n.i+j=n. maybe you’re thinking of the derived tensor product?

"I don't think that's true..." - what don't you think is true? I guess you mean the claim that the internal hom of two bounded chain complexes can be unbounded? Yeah, I don't see how that could be true, not with the usual tensor product of chain complexes.
Right, that’s what I meant, sorry!

To be clear, by 'bounded' I mean 'bounded below'. The ones that Dold-Kan applies to.

I agree that the tensor product of bounded complexes is bounded, but I don't think that's true for the internal hom. The nLab says it's given by

[X,Y]n=∏i∈ZHom(Xi,Yi+n)[X,Y]_n = \prod_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\mathrm{Hom}(X_i,Y_{i+n})[X,Y]n​=∏i∈Z​Hom(Xi​,Yi+n​)

It looks to me that if XXX and YYY aren't bounded above, then [X,Y][X,Y][X,Y] isn't bounded above or below.

@Kevin Carlson Regarding (1), another way to say that would be that the category of chain complexes is equipped with two inequivalent monoidal products. The usual tensor product and one obtained from translation of the tensor product on simplicial abelian groups along the Dold-Kan correspondence. Can we say concretely what that other one is? My first guess would be the levelwise tensor product.

I'm a bit confused. First, I'd say Dold-Kan applies to "nonnegatively graded" chain complexes: collections of vector spaces XiX_iXi​ for i∈Ni \in \mathbb{N}i∈N, with maps d:Xi+1→Xid: X_{i+1} \to X_id:Xi+1​→Xi​ obeying d2=0d^2 = 0d2=0. I'd use "bounded below" for a chain complex consisting of vector spaces XiX_iXi​ for i∈Zi \in \mathbb{Z}i∈Z such that Xi={0}X_i = \{0\}Xi​={0} for iii sufficiently large and negative.
So I don't know why we're talking about chain complexes that are "bounded below".
Okay good point

Second, for more or less the same reason, I don't know why you're writing i∈Zi \in \mathbb{Z}i∈Z in this formula:

[X,Y]n=∏i∈ZHom(Xi,Yi+n)[X,Y]_n = \prod_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\mathrm{Hom}(X_i,Y_{i+n})[X,Y]n​=∏i∈Z​Hom(Xi​,Yi+n​)

if we're talking about the chain complexes to which Dold-Kan applies.
I would write i∈Ni \in \mathbb{N}i∈N or i≥0i \ge 0i≥0.
Then the formula for the internal hom would be right, if we're talking about nonnegatively graded chain complexes, and we're assuming n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N as well.

Oscar Cunningham said:

Kevin Carlson Regarding (1), another way to say that would be that the category of chain complexes is equipped with two inequivalent monoidal products. The usual tensor product and one obtained from translation of the tensor product on simplicial abelian groups along the Dold-Kan correspondence. Can we say concretely what that other one is? My first guess would be the levelwise tensor product.

I’m not quite sure what the “usual” one is supposed to be, but I think the commutative monad will induce the levelwise product!

What I call the "usual" tensor product of chain complexes, the one they teach us as children, is

(X⊗Y)i=⨁j+k=iXj⊗Yk (X \otimes Y)_i = \bigoplus_{j+k = i} X_j \otimes Y_k (X⊗Y)i​=⨁j+k=i​Xj​⊗Yk​
Oops, yeah, I misread that as the usual tensor product of simplicial abelian groups. Yeah, probably the tensor on simplicial abelian groups transfers to the level wise tensor on chain complexes.

I always get confused about the one coming from the tensor product of simplicial abelian groups. Is it really just the "levelwise" tensor product of chain complexes:

(X⊠Y)i=Xi⊗Yi (X \boxtimes Y)_i = X_i \otimes Y_i (X⊠Y)i​=Xi​⊗Yi​ ?

The thing is, when getting a chain complex XXX from a simplicial abelian group, in Dold-Kan we don't just say XiX_iXi​ is the abelian group of iii-simplices - that would be too easy. It's the subgroup that's the intersection of the kernels of all but the last face map.

But that's not proving your guess is wrong!
So you might be right, but it's not instantaneously obvious to me.
Yeah, definitely a loose guess though, just because the Dold-Kan map at least just takes a subgroup at one level, so it’s not obvious how you’d do anything more clever.

It's a bit frustrating that in all the wonderful material about this on the nLab, e.g. at monoidal Dold-Kan correspondence, I don't see anything that comes out and describes this other tensor product of nonnegatively graded chain complexes!

And oh no - apparently two ways to turn a simplicial abelian group AAA into a chain complex of abelian groups are both relevant here: the "obvious" way where we let Cn(A)C_n(A)Cn​(A) be just the abelian group of nnn-simplices, and the way I thought was the "clever" way, the normalized Moore complex, where we let Nn(A)N_n(A)Nn​(A) be the intersection of the kernels of all but the last face map!
Both of them give you a functor from simplicial abelian groups to nonnegatively graded chain complexes of abelian groups that's lax monoidal, oplax monoidal, but not strong monoidal, and so on.
So the question of what we get when we transfer the tensor product of simplicial abelian groups to the category of nonnegatively graded chain complexes of abelian groups is actually two questions.

Also just saw what I hadn't remembered, that the quasi-inverse of the normalized Moore complex functor involves a bunch of different levels of the chain complex, so I no longer particularly suspect the transferred structure is just levelwise!

A fun baby version of this problem is to compare

  • categories internal to AbGp\mathsf{AbGp}AbGp

and

  • 2-term chain complexes of abelian groups, A0←dA1A_0 \xleftarrow{d} A_1 A0​d​A1​

A category internal to AbGp\mathsf{AbGp}AbGp has an abelian group of objects C0C_0C0​ and an abelian group of morpisms C1C_1C1​, and source and target maps s,t:C1→C0s, t: C_1 \to C_0s,t:C1​→C0​, etc. To get a 2-term chain complex starting from this (following the normalized Moore complex idea) we let

A0=C0 A_0 = C_0 A0​=C0​
A1=ker⁡(s)⊆C1A_1 = \ker(s) \subseteq C_1A1​=ker(s)⊆C1​
d=t∣A1 d = t|_{A_1}d=t∣A1​​

Interestingly composition in CCC provides no new extra information, so it's okay that this recipe for getting the chain complex ignores composition!

Conversely, to get a category CCC internal to AbGp\mathsf{AbGp}AbGp from a 2-term chain complex A0←dA1A_0 \xleftarrow{d} A_1A0​d​A1​, we let

C0=A0 C_0 = A_0C0​=A0​
C1=A0⊕A1 C_1 = A_0 \oplus A_1C1​=A0​⊕A1​
s(a0,a1)=a0 s(a_0, a_1) = a_0s(a0​,a1​)=a0​
t(a0,a1)=a0+d(a1) t(a_0, a_1) = a_0 + d(a_1) t(a0​,a1​)=a0​+d(a1​)

and we can define composition too, using addition in A1A_1A1​.

The formula C1=A0⊕A1C_1 = A_0 \oplus A_1C1​=A0​⊕A1​ is a baby version of how

the quasi-inverse of the normalized Moore complex functor involves a bunch of different levels of the chain complex

The whole tensor product issue shows up in this baby version of the story, too. In some ways it's simpler than version for arbitrary positively graded chain complexes, but the restriction to 2-term chain complexes adds extra wrinkles, too.

John Baez said:

What I call the "usual" tensor product of chain complexes, the one they teach us as children, is

(X⊗Y)i=⨁j+k=iXj⊗Yk (X \otimes Y)_i = \bigoplus_{j+k = i} X_j \otimes Y_k (X⊗Y)i​=⨁j+k=i​Xj​⊗Yk​

this corresponds to the join of simplicial sets, i.e. day convolution

This is worth getting to know well, especially since it holds a subtlety: the equivalence between chain complexes in AbGp and simplicial abelian groups is both lax monoidal and oplax monoidal in both directions, but not strong monoidal.

I've been meaning to write this up in detail but imo the usual story about Dold-Kan is not the most categorically natural one. I would go so far as to say that the historical convention chosen by Moore for the Moore normalization functor is in some sense "wrong" - not wrong in an objective sense but just an unfortunate convention in the sense that we get weird theorems which are difficult to interpret (both lax and oplax monoidal but not strong monoidal? what?)
Let me advocate my alternative story of the Dold-Kan correspondence.
The augmented simplex category is distinguished up to isomorphism by its universal property: it is the free strict monoidal category with distinguished monoid.
If CCC is a small category, then the presheaf category C^\hat{C}C^ is distinguished up to equivalence by its universal property: it is the free cocompletion of CCC.

When C=ΔC=\DeltaC=Δ, these two universal properties combine with each other in a pleasing way: the category of augmented simplicial sets is the free cocomplete monoidal category with distinguished monoid. What I mean by this is that SSet\mathbf{SSet}SSet (I will here consider augmented simplicial sets) is cocomplete, equipped with a tensor product (Day convolution, aka the join of simplicial sets) which is cocontinuous in both arguments, and it is equipped with a distinguished monoid (the terminal object). It is universal among cocomplete categories equipped with a monoidal product, cocontinuous in both arguments, and a distinguished monoid. The most important such category is the category Top\mathbf{Top}Top whose tensor product is the topological join X∗Y=X∐X×I×Y∐Y/∼X\ast Y = X\coprod X\times I \times Y\coprod Y/\simX∗Y=X∐X×I×Y∐Y/∼, where x∼(x,0,y)x\sim (x,0, y)x∼(x,0,y) and (x,1,y)∼y(x,1,y)\sim y(x,1,y)∼y and whose distinguished monoid is the singleton.

One checks that this all goes through in the Ab\mathbf{Ab}Ab-enriched setting, and that the category of simplicial Abelian groups is universal in the 2-category Ab−cat\mathbf{Ab}-\mathbf{cat}Ab−cat among cocomplete categories (in the Ab-enriched sense, so including the tensor product of an object with an Abelian group) equipped with a tensor product cocontinuous in both arguments (the join again, but with Cartesian product of sets replaced by tensor product of Abelian groups.) The distinguished monoid is the constant presheaf at Z\mathbb{Z}Z

The category of chain complexes of Abelian groups is also an Ab-enriched category with a tensor product (the usual tensor product of chain complexes mentioned earlier in this thread) and it has a distinguished monoid, 0→Z→Z→00\to \mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}\to 00→Z→Z→0, concentrated in degrees zero and one. Now, note that if this were concentrated in degrees 0 and -1 instead of 0 and 1, we might squint our eyes and say, the generator in degree 0 corresponds to a point, and the generator in degree -1 corresponds to the empty simplex - one might take it or leave it out depending on convention, and this leads to the distinction between ordinary homology and reduced homology. However, our complex is concentrated in degrees 0 and 1, not 0 and -1. This distinction can be explained by choosing to index by the number of vertices in the simplex, not the dimension of the simplex - the 0 simplex is the convex hull of 1 point, so we place the generator in dimension 1. the -1 simplex is the convex hull with zero points, so we place the generator in dimension 0.
So, I prefer to think of this complex 0→Z→Z→00\to\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}\to 00→Z→Z→0 as being a formal analogue of the singleton space.

By the universal property of SAb\mathbf{SAb}SAb in Ab−cat\mathbf{Ab}-\mathbf{cat}Ab−cat which we stated previously, there is an Ab-enriched cocontinuous functor preserving the monoidal product and the distinguished monoid, unique up to natural isomorphism, F:SAb→Ch(Ab)F : \mathbf{SAb}\to \mathbf{Ch}(\mathbf{Ab})F:SAb→Ch(Ab), and it turns out that this functor is an equivalence of categories

Now, I wish we could say "and this is precisely the Dold-Kan correspondence!"

However, the devil is in the details. I have described an equivalence between augmented simplicial Abelian groups and chain complexes, not between unaugmented simplicial Abelian groups and chain complexes.

The functor FFF I have described is, by construction, a (strong) monoidal equivalence

The difference is in the indexing, and that is a big difference indeed. Reindexing the groups of a chain complex by one is called "suspension" and it is a nontrivial geometric operation. In particular, suspension gives an equivalence between Ch≥0(Ab)\mathbf{Ch}_{\geq 0}(\mathbf{Ab})Ch≥0​(Ab) and Ch≥1(Ab)\mathbf{Ch}_{\geq 1}(\mathbf{Ab})Ch≥1​(Ab), but not a monoidal equivalence.

The usual Dold-Kan correspondence can be described as follows:

  1. Given an unaugmented simplicial Abelian group, regard it as an augmented simplicial Abelian group by extending it with the zero group as augmentation
  2. Apply the functor FFF described above
  3. Shift everything down by one

If we return to John's original comment that

the equivalence is... both lax monoidal and oplax monoidal in both directions, but not strong monoidal.

I would prefer to say this as follows:
The suspension functor Σ:(SAb,⊠,Z)→(SAb,⊗Day,1Day)\Sigma : (\mathbf{SAb}, \boxtimes, \mathbb{Z}) \to (\mathbf{SAb}, \otimes_{\mathbf{Day}}, \mathbf{1}_{\mathbf{Day}})Σ:(SAb,⊠,Z)→(SAb,⊗Day​,1Day​) is lax monoidal and oplax monoidal, but not strong monoidal.

(1Day\mathbf{1}_{\mathbf{Day}}1Day​ is the augmented simplicial Abelian group which is Z\mathbb{Z}Z in degree zero and 000 in all higher degrees.)

There is no need to bring chain complexes into it anywhere, in my view - fundamentally, this is a theorem about the relationship between the degreewise tensor product (which corresponds geometrically to the Cartesian product of simplicial complexes) and the "total" tensor product (which corresponds geometrically to the join of simplicial complexes)

And this theorem clearly corresponds to standard theorems in topology -
https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/3709847/prove-that-the-reduced-join-of-2-spaces-is-homeomorphic-to-the-reduced-suspensio
the reduced join of two spaces is homeomorphic to the reduced suspension of their smash product.

John Baez said:

And oh no - apparently two ways to turn a simplicial abelian group AAA into a chain complex of abelian groups are both relevant here: the "obvious" way where we let Cn(A)C_n(A)Cn​(A) be just the abelian group of nnn-simplices, and the way I thought was the "clever" way, the normalized Moore complex, where we let Nn(A)N_n(A)Nn​(A) be the intersection of the kernels of all but the last face map!

this "obvious" way is the categorically natural way to convert an augmented semisimplicial Abelian group into a chain complex. The category of semisimplicial Abelian groups is the free Ab-enriched cocomplete monoidal category with a distinguished pointed object e:1Day→Ze : 1_{\mathbf{Day}}\to\mathbb{Z}e:1Day​→Z. If you apply this universal property to SSAb→Ch(Ab)\mathbf{SSAb}\to \mathbf{Ch(Ab)}SSAb→Ch(Ab) you get the alternating sum of face maps functor. By construction this is again strong monoidal with respect to the Day convolution.

Once you realize this, it seems somewhat obvious that the alternating sums definition is not the "right" one for a simplicial Abelian group as it doesn't incorporate the degeneracies. The Moore complex, on the other hand, can be defined in terms of quotienting out by the degeneracies, which makes it feel a bit more natural.

Wow, this is a great story, @Patrick Nicodemus - a substantial improvement on all the stories I've heard about this subject. It's so much nicer to use universal properties, as you're doing, rather than to check tricky formulas. And admitting the importance of how the augmented simplex category is the free monoidal category on a monoid object seems like the way to use this. But this introduces a reindexing issue (since the augmented simplex category has "(-1)-simplices" in the usual numbering system). But you address this using suspension.

Patrick Nicodemus said:

And this theorem clearly corresponds to standard theorems in topology -
https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/3709847/prove-that-the-reduced-join-of-2-spaces-is-homeomorphic-to-the-reduced-suspensio
the reduced join of two spaces is homeomorphic to the reduced suspension of their smash product.

I'll need to think about that and see if I can visualize it. I'm not sure I ever thought about the reduced [[join of topological spaces]]. I'm giving a link that explains the join, but not the reduced join. Heuristically we get the join X⋆YX \star YX⋆Y of XXX and YYY, by drawing an interval between each point of XXX and each point of YYY, and then I guess we get the reduced join by taking X⋆YX \star YX⋆Y and collapsing the original copy of XXX to a point and collapsing the original copy of YYY to a point.

Anyway, I hope you make your story available to the world at large. If writing a paper is enough work that you tend to put it off, you could at least put what you've just written now into the nLab. (You might not want to do that.) It would be great if the rather intricate story about how the [[Eilenberg-Zilber map]] is the laxator for the functor from simplicial abelian groups to chain complexes, and how the [[Alexander-Whitney map]] is the oplaxator, could be clarified using your thoughts. But I think if you get the basic ideas out into the world, other people may use them to clarify these related constructions.

Yes, it is indeed a very nice story; thank you very much Patrick. In spirit, in its emphasis on monoidal doctrines that distribute over free cocompletion (in an enriched sense), it reminds me very much of the story behind Kelly's On the Operads of J.P. May, as Kelly taught me in brilliantly clear form when I was a post-doc at Macquarie.

Just a quick note on Eilenberg Zilber and Alexander Whitney while we are still having this conversation.
I'm going off old memories right now but I'll provide details when I get a chance. Hopefully this is correct. Let Z[Δ]\mathbb{Z}[\Delta]Z[Δ] be the free Ab enriched category on the augmented simplex category. (Take free Abelian groups on Hom sets and extend composition by bilinearity). By Yoneda it should be clear that many natural operations on presheaves can be reduced to operations in this category. In particular it should be the case that Eilenberg-Zilber and Alexander-Whitney should both be describable somehow without using any presheaves. I believe it is something like

Let PPP be the functor Z[Δ]⊗Z[Δ]→Z[Δ]⊗Z[Δ]\mathbb{Z}[\Delta]\otimes \mathbb{Z}[\Delta]\to\mathbb{Z}[\Delta]\otimes \mathbb{Z}[\Delta]Z[Δ]⊗Z[Δ]→Z[Δ]⊗Z[Δ]
defined by (n,m)↦(n+m,n+m)(n,m)\mapsto (n+m,n+m)(n,m)↦(n+m,n+m).

I believe that the identity functor is a retract (in the category of functors and additive natural transformations) of PPP.
One of the natural transformations here is essentially the Eilenberg-Zilber map and the other is the Alexander Whitney map.

Other interesting things about chain complexes also can be observed in this category for Yoneda reasons. For example Z[Δ]\mathbb{Z}[\Delta]Z[Δ] must be a symmetric monoidal category because the tensor product of chain complexes is symmetric monoidal. Even though Δ\DeltaΔ is of course not symmetric monoidal, we can define a symmetry isomorphism by taking formal linear combinations.
(Obviously some missing details here - where does the suspension enter into it? I have to do some work here.)
but thanks for the encouragement! I'll try to write it up rigorously/completely!

John will also remember that we made use of a similar chain complex to 0→Z→Z→00 \to \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z} \to 00→Z→Z→0 in section 7 of our Schur functors paper, but with Z\mathbb{Z}Z replaced by the generator xxx of the free 2-rig generated by xxx (starting around page 48).

The suspension, and conjugation by suspension, plays an important role in many constructions. If one thinks of a superspace (C0,C1)(C_0, C_1)(C0​,C1​) or a dg-superspace (C0,C1,∂0,∂1)(C_0, C_1, \partial_0, \partial_1)(C0​,C1​,∂0​,∂1​) as a proxy for a virtual construction "C0−C1C_0 - C_1C0​−C1​" [where in the dg-case one has a quasi-isomorphism (C0,C1)∼(H0(C),H1(C))(C_0, C_1) \sim (H_0(C), H_1(C))(C0​,C1​)∼(H0​(C),H1​(C)) -- cf. Euler's formula "H0−H1=C0−C1H_0 - H_1 = C_0 - C_1H0​−H1​=C0​−C1​", a key principle in our section 7] then Z2\mathbb{Z}_2Z2​-graded suspension (C0,C1)↦(C1,C0)(C_0, C_1) \mapsto (C_1, C_0)(C0​,C1​)↦(C1​,C0​) plays the role of "taking negatives" at the virtual level. (That is the sort of categorified explanation for how to think of negatives, or "co-negation" for the biring we were constructing.)

One of my favorite papers where this is utilized is Joyal's Foncteurs Analytiques et Especes de Structures (I'm skipping any accent marks; sorry) where he first shows how to combinatorially invert the linear (i.e. vector space-enriched) species exp⁡(X)−1\exp(X) - 1exp(X)−1 to a species log⁡(1+X)\log(1+X)log(1+X), and then replaces XXX by −X-X−X by applying suspension (this is closely tied to his "rule of signs", which involves tensoring with a sign representation). Actually he conjugates log⁡(1+X)\log(1+X)log(1+X) by suspension, so as to obtain −log⁡(1−X)-\log(1-X)−log(1−X). In other words, he gives a linear species meaning to

log⁡11−X=log⁡(1+X+X2+…)=log⁡TX\log \frac1{1-X} = \log (1+X + X^2 + \ldots) = \log TXlog1−X1​=log(1+X+X2+…)=logTX

where TXTXTX is the tensor algebra (free monoid) on XXX. The species log⁡TX\log TXlogTX he winds up with solves for Lie(X)\mathrm{Lie}(X)Lie(X), the free Lie algebra on XXX, from the composition of species isomorphisms

TX≅ULie(X)≅SymLie(X)≅exp⁡(Lie(X)).TX \cong \mathcal{U} \mathrm{Lie}(X) \cong \mathrm{Sym} \mathrm{Lie}(X) \cong \exp(\mathrm{Lie}(X)).TX≅ULie(X)≅SymLie(X)≅exp(Lie(X)).

Where

  • The first isomorphism is just by composing left adjoints to forgetful functors, one from algebra objects in linear species to Lie algebra objects in linear species;

  • The second isomorphism is meant to be interpreted as an isomorphism between graded vector spaces, of Poincare-Birkhoff-Witt theorem type, coming from the evident filtered vector spaces for the universal enveloping algebra U\mathcal{U}U and the symmetric algebra Sym\mathrm{Sym}Sym;

  • The third isomorphism is by viewing SymnX\mathrm{Sym}^n XSymnX as the quotient Xn/n!X^n/\mathbf{n!}Xn/n! of a tensor power modulo the action of the symmetric group n!:=Sn\mathbf{n!} := S_nn!:=Sn​.

Anyway, Joyal computes in this way the species underlying the Lie algebra operad Lie(X)\mathrm{Lie}(X)Lie(X) as this log⁡TX\log TXlogTX interpreted in this ingenious way, where the result is that Lie(X)[n]\mathrm{Lie}(X)[n]Lie(X)[n], as a linear SnS_nSn​-representation, is isomorphic to the top homology group H∗H^\astH∗ of a partition lattice Π(n)−{⊥,⊤}\Pi(n) - \{\bot, \top\}Π(n)−{⊥,⊤} for a set with nnn elements (all other homology groups being zero), twisted by a determinant or sign representation,

Lie(X)[n]=(−1)n+1H∗(Π(n))⊗signSn,\mathrm{Lie}(X)[n] = (-1)^{n+1} H^\ast(\Pi(n)) \otimes \mathrm{sign}_{S_n},Lie(X)[n]=(−1)n+1H∗(Π(n))⊗signSn​​,

if I'm remembering correctly.
